era_context: The late 17th century, a period of European state consolidation, colonial expansion, and scientific advancement. The Franco-Dutch War (1672–78) is ongoing. The Ottoman Empire remains a major power but faces increasing European pressure. China's Kangxi Emperor is consolidating Qing control. England is politically divided between Whigs and Tories. The Scientific Revolution is advancing. Colonial competition in North America is intensifying.

events.title: Scanian War — Beginning of conflict between Sweden and Denmark-Norway-Brandenburg coalition
events.description: The Scanian War (1675–79) began in 1675 as a conflict between Sweden under King Charles XI and a coalition of Denmark-Norway and Brandenburg. The war centered on control of Scania and other Baltic territories. Sweden had gained Scania from Denmark in the Treaty of Roskilde (1658) but Denmark sought to recover it. The war would last four years and involve significant military campaigns, including the Battle of Lund (1676). It would conclude with the Peace of Lund (1679), which confirmed Swedish control of Scania. The war demonstrated the military capabilities of the Swedish state but also the costs of maintaining great power status.

sources.name: Swedish military records and campaign reports

events.certainty_note: War's beginning in 1675 is precisely dated and extensively documented in Scandinavian archives.

events.title: King Philip's War — Beginning of major conflict in New England
events.description: King Philip's War (1675–78) began in 1675 as a major conflict between English colonists and Native American forces led by Metacom (King Philip) of the Wampanoag Confederacy. The war was one of the most destructive conflicts in colonial North American history, with significant casualties on both sides. It was triggered by English encroachment on Native American lands and disputes over colonial authority. The war would last three years and would result in the decimation of Native American populations in New England and the consolidation of English colonial control. It had profound consequences for colonial settlement patterns and Native American societies.

sources.name: Native American oral traditions, recorded later

events.certainty_note: War's beginning in 1675 is precisely dated in colonial records.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor — Consolidation of power and suppression of Three Feudatories revolt
events.description: The Kangxi Emperor faced the Revolt of the Three Feudatories (1673–81) in 1675. The revolt, led by Wu Sangui, Geng Jingzhong, and Zheng Keshuang, threatened Qing control of southern China. Kangxi had ordered the reduction of the feudatories' power in 1673, which triggered the revolt. By 1675, the Qing military was mobilizing for a major campaign to suppress the revolt. Kangxi's strategy combined military pressure with diplomatic overtures and administrative reforms. The final suppression would come in 1681. This period saw significant military innovation and the development of Qing military capabilities.

sources.name: Kangxi's imperial edicts and military reports
Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty (Qing Shilu)
Military campaign records and battle reports

events.certainty_note: Imperial records and military documentation confirm the revolt's continuation in 1675.

events.title: Franco-Dutch War — Continued military operations and French advances
events.description: The Franco-Dutch War (1672–78) continued in 1675 with ongoing military campaigns. French forces under Louis XIV pressed their advantage against the Dutch Republic and Spanish Netherlands. The war had begun with the French invasion of the Dutch Republic in 1672 but had evolved into a broader European conflict. By 1675, both sides were engaged in military operations and diplomatic maneuvering. The war demonstrated the military superiority of France under Louis XIV and the decline of Dutch military power. Peace negotiations would begin in 1676 and lead to the Treaty of Nijmegen (1678).

sources.name: French military records and campaign reports

events.certainty_note: Military records and diplomatic correspondence are well-documented.

events.title: Death of Turenne — Major French military commander
events.description: Henri de la Tour d'Auvergne, Vicomte de Turenne, one of France's greatest military commanders, was killed in action on July 27, 1675, during the Franco-Dutch War. Turenne had served Louis XIV for decades and was renowned for his military innovations and strategic brilliance. His death was a significant loss to the French military, though it did not fundamentally alter the course of the war. Turenne's legacy influenced military thought for centuries, and his death marked the end of an era of great military commanders.

sources.name: French military records and contemporary accounts

events.certainty_note: Turenne's death on July 27, 1675, is precisely dated and extensively documented.

events.title: Ottoman Empire — Consolidation under Kara Mustafa Pasha
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Sultan Mehmed IV continued consolidation under the leadership of Grand Vizier Kara Mustafa Pasha in 1675. Kara Mustafa had become Grand Vizier in 1676 (or 1675 depending on source), and he would lead the Ottoman Empire through a period of military expansion and administrative reform. He would be responsible for the Ottoman invasion of Hungary and the siege of Vienna (1683). In 1675, the Ottoman Empire was still a major power, though facing increasing pressure from European rivals, particularly the Habsburgs and Russia.
key_figures: Kara Mustafa Pasha (Ottoman Grand Vizier, appointed 1676)

sources.name: European diplomatic records regarding Ottoman affairs

events.certainty_note: Ottoman records document the period; the exact dating of Kara Mustafa's appointment is sometimes disputed (1675 vs. 1676).

disconfirming_evidence: The Scanian War is sometimes portrayed as a clear Swedish victory; modern scholarship emphasizes that while Sweden retained Scania, the war was costly and inconclusive, and it marked the beginning of Swedish decline as a great power. King Philip's War is sometimes portrayed as a simple English victory; in reality, the conflict was more complex, with significant English casualties and ongoing Native American resistance. Turenne's death is sometimes portrayed as a major turning point in the Franco-Dutch War; modern scholarship emphasizes that while significant, it did not fundamentally alter the course of the war, which was already moving toward peace. The dating of Kara Mustafa Pasha's appointment as Grand Vizier is disputed in sources; some sources place it in 1675, others in 1676.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1675 is rich for European and East Asian events. European sources are predominantly elite and court-centric, with extensive military and diplomatic records. Chinese sources benefit from Confucian emphasis on record-keeping but are court-centric. Colonial North American sources are more abundant for this year due to King Philip's War, but are heavily English-centric; Native American perspectives are largely absent or mediated through English interpretation. Ottoman sources are less abundant in Western archives but are preserved in Istanbul. Sub-Saharan African and Pacific Island events are largely absent from surviving documentation. The dating of some Ottoman administrative changes (such as Kara Mustafa's appointment) is sometimes uncertain due to differences between Ottoman and European calendar systems and record-keeping practices.

graph_edges.note: Scanian War beginning in 1675 led to Peace of Lund in 1679.
King Philip's War beginning in 1675 led to war's conclusion in 1678.
Three Feudatories revolt in 1675 led to final suppression in 1681.
Franco-Dutch War operations in 1675 led to Treaty of Nijmegen in 1678.
Turenne's death in 1675 occurred during Franco-Dutch War, which concluded with Treaty of Nijmegen in 1678.
Ottoman consolidation under Kara Mustafa in 1675 led to siege of Vienna in 1683.
